core_intuitions: Parametric memory has hard walls (staleness, finite capacity, no attribution); RAG swaps 'answer from memory' for 'answer conditioned on freshly retrieved docs': P(y|q) becomes P(y|q, D_q), and updating knowledge means re-indexing, not re-training.
RAG is a five-stage pipeline split across time: OFFLINE chunk to embed to index builds the store once/on-update; ONLINE retrieve (optionally rerank) to generate runs per query against that store.

Retrieval and reranking are two DIFFERENT models: a bi-encoder embeds query and document independently (fast, vectors precomputed offline, coarse) to cast a wide net; a cross-encoder scores query+document jointly (slow, per-pair, fine-grained) to precisely re-order the shortlist.

Naive RAG fails in two arenas: RETRIEVAL failures (semantic/keyword mismatch, bad chunk boundaries, top-k missing multi-hop evidence, stale index) and CONTEXT-UTILIZATION failures (lost-in-the-middle, hallucination despite context, redundant near-duplicate chunks).

RAGAS disaggregates quality into a retrieval side (context precision/recall) and a generation side (faithfulness = fraction of answer claims supported by context; answer relevance = the answer actually addresses the query) - all scored by an LLM judge with no human labels.

Hybrid search (BM25 sparse + dense) fused by Reciprocal Rank Fusion and cross-encoder reranking are the two highest-leverage upgrades over naive RAG; long-context vs RAG is a corpus-size/freshness/cost tradeoff and the two compose (iterative RAG).

existing.name: rag-arch-pipeline-five-stages
existing.issue: Rendering is clean and clear in both themes and correctly conveys the offline/online split - but the ONLINE row labels every one of its four boxes 'Stage 4' (Stage 4 - Query, Stage 4 - Embed, Stage 4 - Retrieve, Stage 4 - Rerank), which reads like an error and muddies the five-stage story. 'Stage 4 - Embed' also visually collides with the offline 'Stage 2 - Embed'. Minor secondary nit: the light-blue 'Vector DB' label sits on a light-blue cylinder gradient with weak contrast.
existing.improvement: Renumber the online row so the five stages read cleanly: keep the query-side boxes as un-numbered prep ('Query' and 'Embed query'), label only the retrieval box 'Stage 4 - Retrieve', mark the reranker 'Stage 4 - Rerank (optional)', and keep 'Stage 5 - Generate'. Bump the 'Vector DB' label contrast against the cylinder. Leave the rest as-is.

existing.name: rag-pipeline
existing.issue: Good concrete worked-trace (real scores 0.83/0.79/0.74, k=3, numbered citations [1][2][3], grounded answer tracing back to cited chunks) that complements the abstract five-stages figure by carrying the attribution/grounding intuition. BUT it has genuine defects in both themes: (1) right-edge TEXT CLIPPING - 'answer from context o[nly]' is cut off, the LLM answer text 'It tiles Q,K,V into SRAM blocks an[d]' and 'never materializes the N x N matri[x]' are cut off, and the bottom line 'claims trace back to cited chunks' overruns the bottom of the viewBox; (2) TOFU / missing-glyph boxes rendering as squares in several labels ('bi-encoder [box]', 'q [box] R_h', 'LLM [box] grounded answer').
existing.improvement: Widen the viewBox (or shrink the Augment/LLM-answer font and wrap text) so every character of the prompt-template box and the LLM answer box sits fully inside the frame; ensure the bottom 'claims trace back to cited chunks' line is inside the viewBox. Replace all tofu glyphs with characters that render (use ASCII arrows '->' or plain unicode that the embed font supports, 'in' or the math member sign only if it renders, 'R^h'). Keep the concept and the traced numbers unchanged.

new_figures.name: bi-encoder-vs-cross-encoder
new_figures.concept: The two relevance-scoring architectures behind retrieve-then-rerank: a bi-encoder encodes query and document separately then compares vectors; a cross-encoder feeds query+document through one tower with joint attention and emits a single score.
new_figures.rationale: This distinction is the single most-referenced conceptual pivot in the chapter - it appears in Stage 4, in the 'no cross-encoder reranking' failure mode, in the latency worked example, and in the key takeaways ('highest-leverage improvements') - yet neither existing figure visualizes WHY one is fast-but-coarse and the other accurate-but-expensive. Making the independent-vs-joint encoding visible is what makes retrieve-then-rerank click.
new_figures.brief: Two side-by-side panels sharing a bottom strip. LEFT PANEL titled 'Bi-encoder (retriever)': draw two SEPARATE identical encoder towers stacked vertically - top tower input 'query q', bottom tower input 'document d' - each tower a small labeled 'Encoder' block emitting its own vector (render each output as a short column of ~4 cells or an arrow to a labeled dot 'q in R^d' / 'v in R^d'). The two vectors converge on a similarity node 'cos(q, v)' that outputs one scalar score. Callouts: 'encodes query and doc INDEPENDENTLY', 'doc vectors precomputed and indexed OFFLINE', 'query: one encode + fast ANN over millions', 'coarse - no query-doc interaction'. RIGHT PANEL titled 'Cross-encoder (reranker)': a SINGLE tower whose input is the concatenation '[query]  [SEP]  [document]'; show the two token groups feeding one 'Encoder' block and draw a few attention arcs connecting query tokens to doc tokens (to convey JOINT attention), ending in a classification head that emits one 'relevance score'. Callouts: 'query and doc attend JOINTLY', 'fine-grained token interaction', 'must run once PER (query, doc) pair at query time', 'accurate but O(candidates) forward passes'. BOTTOM STRIP spanning both panels: a mini left-to-right pipeline 'millions of docs  ->  [bi-encoder ANN: wide + cheap]  ->  top-20 candidates  ->  [cross-encoder: narrow + precise]  ->  top-3', which shows why the cheap coarse net comes first and the expensive precise filter second. Use one theme accent for the fast/coarse path and a second accent for the precise path; theme-safe (no hardcoded primaries), only the small integers 20 and 3, invent no benchmark numbers. Everything legible and fully inside the viewBox, clear reading order left-to-right then bottom.

new_figures.name: ragas-metric-decomposition
new_figures.concept: RAGAS as a triangle over Query, Retrieved Context, and Answer, where each edge is probed by a specific metric, and the metrics group into retrieval-quality vs generation-quality buckets.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's central eval message is that quality must be DISAGGREGATED into retrieval and generation, and that each of the three/four metrics tests a different edge of the query-context-answer relationship. A static triad makes it obvious which metric catches which failure (e.g. faithful-but-irrelevant vs relevant-but-hallucinated) far better than the prose formulas alone. This is the eval home chapter, so it belongs here.
new_figures.placement: ## RAG Evaluation: RAGAS and the Three Metrics
new_figures.brief: Draw a triangle with three labeled nodes: top 'Query q', bottom-left 'Retrieved Context D_q' (rendered as a small stack of 3 chunk cards), bottom-right 'Answer y'. Label each edge with the metric that probes it: (1) EDGE Query <-> Context = 'Context Precision / Recall' with subtext 'are the retrieved chunks the right ones?  precision = useful / k,  recall = found / relevant' - tag this edge as the RETRIEVAL side. (2) EDGE Context -> Answer = 'Faithfulness' with subtext 'is every claim in the answer supported by the context?'; attach a small inset that decomposes the answer into 3 atomic claim chips, 2 marked with a check (supported) and 1 with a cross (unsupported), and show the fraction 'score = 2/3' beside it to make the formula concrete. (3) EDGE Query <-> Answer = 'Answer Relevance' with subtext 'does the answer actually address the question?'; attach a small inset showing 'answer -> regenerate hypothetical questions q1, q2, q3 -> cosine back to q'. Visually cluster the metrics into TWO buckets with a light band or label: 'RETRIEVAL QUALITY (context precision / recall)' and 'GENERATION QUALITY (faithfulness, answer relevance)', reinforcing the disaggregation point. Add a small footer note 'all scored by an LLM judge - no human labels'. Theme-safe accents (one for retrieval bucket, one for generation bucket); the only numbers are the illustrative 2-of-3 supported claims - invent NO benchmark scores. Keep all insets and text inside the viewBox with no overlap.

new_figures.name: hybrid-rrf-fusion
new_figures.concept: Hybrid search fuses a sparse BM25 (keyword) ranking and a dense (semantic) ranking via Reciprocal Rank Fusion, so documents ranked well by either signal survive and documents ranked well by BOTH get boosted.
new_figures.rationale: Hybrid + RRF is named in the key takeaways as one of the two highest-leverage upgrades over naive RAG, and the RRF formula is presented in-chapter, but nothing visualizes it. The intuition - two complementary rankings (exact keywords vs semantics) merged by reciprocal rank so agreement bubbles to the top while each signal's unique catches still surface - is exactly the kind of mechanism a static figure nails.
new_figures.brief: Left-to-right layout. TWO input ranked columns for the SAME query. LEFT column header 'BM25  (sparse - exact keywords)' listing document ids in rank order, e.g. rank1 d3, rank2 d1, rank3 d7, rank4 d2. RIGHT column header 'Dense  (semantic - embeddings)' listing rank1 d1, rank2 d5, rank3 d3, rank4 d8. Beside each ranked slot draw a small decreasing bar representing its contribution weight 1/(k+rank), and label the constant 'k = 60' once. MIDDLE fusion node labeled 'RRF(d) = sum of 1/(k + rank)' that pulls the same doc id from both columns. RIGHT output column header 'Fused ranking' showing the merged order where a doc that appeared HIGH IN BOTH lists (d1 and d3) rises to the top - annotate 'agreement across both signals -> boosted'. Also draw two thin call-out arrows: one from a BM25-only hit (e.g. d7, a rare exact keyword the dense model missed) showing it still survives into the fused list, and one from a dense-only hit (e.g. d5, a paraphrase BM25 missed) also surviving - annotate 'complementary strengths: keywords + meaning'. Use the real constant k=60 and only small illustrative doc ids/ranks; invent no benchmark scores. Theme-safe (one accent for sparse, one for dense, a neutral for fused). Clear left-to-right reading order, all bars and labels inside the viewBox.

summary: This concept-rich chapter currently has only two figures, both about pipeline FLOW. The abstract five-stages figure is strong (keep, with one refine: it labels four separate online boxes all 'Stage 4', which reads like an error - renumber so the five stages read cleanly). The concrete rag-pipeline trace is a good complement but ships with real defects - right-edge text clipping in the prompt-template and LLM-answer boxes and several tofu/missing-glyph squares - so it needs a refine to fit text in the viewBox and swap non-rendering glyphs. Three core intuitions are entirely unserved by any figure, and I propose one foundational static figure for each: (P1) bi-encoder vs cross-encoder, the independent-vs-joint encoding distinction that is the chapter's most-referenced pivot; (P1) a RAGAS query-context-answer triangle that makes the retrieval-vs-generation metric decomposition visible; and (P2) a hybrid BM25+dense RRF-fusion diagram for the highest-leverage retrieval upgrade whose formula already sits in the text.
